concepts.title: Conservative forces
concepts.conceptBody:
Conservative force 

It is a force, work done by which does not depend on the path along which body is moved, but depends on initial and final positions only.

concepts.title: Non Conservative forces
concepts.conceptBody:
Non-conservative force 

Work done by the force depends on the path followed along which the particle moves.

Example friction, viscous force

$\oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\vec{F}}_{\text{nc}}}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}\ne 0$

Examples 

  • Non-conservative force 

       

 $\mathrm{dW}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$

$=-\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{ds}$

$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{k}} \int \mathrm{ds}$

$\Delta \mathrm{W}=-\mu \mathrm{mgs} \text { depends on path followed. }$

concepts.title: Kinetic Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Kinetic Energy 

Kinetic energy may be described as energy due to motion.

The kinetic energy of a body may be defined as the amount of work it can do before being brought to rest.

For example when a hammer is used to knock in a nail, work is done on the nail by the hammer and hence the hammer must have possessed energy.

Formulae for kinetic energy 

Let a body of mass $\text{m}$ moving with speed $\text{v}$ be brought to rest with uniform deceleration by a constant force $\text{F}$ over a distance $\text{s}$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{v}^{2}}={{u}^{2}}+2as \\ {} & 0={{u}^{2}}+2as \\ {} & s=\frac{{{v}^{2}}}{2a} \\ \end{array}$

And work done is given by $\text { Work done }=\text { force distance }$

$=\mathrm{Fs}$

$=\mathrm{F} \frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{2 \mathrm{a}}$

The force is $\text{F}=$ ma so

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~=\text{ma}\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{2\text{a}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

This kinetic energy is given by kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Potential Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Potential Energy 

The energy possessed by a body or system by virtue of its position or configuration is known as the potential energy. For example, a block attached to a compressed or elongated spring possesses some energy called elastic potential energy. This block has a capacity to do work. Similarly, a stone when released from a certain height also has energy in the form of gravitational potential energy. Two charged particles kept at certain distance has electric potential energy.

Regarding the potential energy it is important to note that it is defined for a conservative force field only. For non-conservative forces it has no meaning. The change in potential energy (dU) of a system corresponding to a conservative internal force is given by

$\text{dU}=-\vec{F}\cdot \vec{d}\text{r}=-\text{dW }\left( \text{F}=-\frac{\text{d}U}{\text{dr}} \right)$

Or $\int \vec{F}\cdot \vec{d}\text{r }\text{ or }{{\text{U}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{U}}_{\text{i}}}=\mathop{\int }_{{{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{i}}}}^{{{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{f}}}}\vec{F}\cdot \vec{d} \text{r}$

We generally choose the reference point at infinity and assume potential energy to be zero there, i.e. if we take ${{r}_{i}}=\infty$ (infinite) and ${{U}_{i}}=0$ then we can write

\(U=-\int_{\propto}^{0} \vec{r} \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} r=-W\)

or potential energy of a body or system is the negative of work done by the conservative forces in bringing it from infinity to the present position.

Regarding the potential energy it is worth noting that:

  1. Potential energy can be defined only for conservative forces and it should be considered to be a property of the entire system rather than assigning it to any specific particle.
  2. Potential energy depends on frame of reference.

concepts.title: Work Energy Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
Work Energy Theorem

When a body with mass $m$ has its speed increased from $u$ to $v$ in a distance $s$ by a constant force $\text{F}$ which produces an acceleration a, then from we known $\mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as}$

\(\frac{1}{2} v^2-\frac{1}{2} u^2=a s\)

multiplying this by $\text{m}$ give an expression of the increase in kinetic energy (the difference in kinetic energy at the end and the start).

$\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{u}}^{2}}=\text{mas}$

Thus since $\text{F}=\text{ma}$

$\text{ increase }\text{ in }\text{ kinetic }\text{ energy }=\text{Fs}$

but also we know

$\text{ Fs }=\text{ work }\text{ done }$

So the relationship between kinetic energy can be summed up as

Work done by forces acting on a body = change of kinetic energy in the body.

concepts.title: Elastic Potential Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Elastic Potential Energy 

We have seen that the work done by the spring force (of course conservative for an ideal spring) is $-\frac{1}{2}\text{k}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$ when the spring is stretched or compressed by an amount $\text{x}$ from its unstretched

position. Thus,

$\text{U}=-\text{W}=-\left( -\frac{1}{2}\text{k}{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)$

Or $\text{U}=\frac{1}{2}\text{k}{{\text{x}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{k}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ spring }\!\!~\!\!\text{ constsant }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

Note that elastic potential energy is always positive.

concepts.title: Gravitational Potential Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential Energy

The gravitational potential energy of two particles of masses ${{m}_{1}}$ and ${{m}_{2}}$ separated by a distance $r$ is given by

\(\mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{G} \frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{r}}\left\{\begin{array}{l}\text { No need to remember these } \\ \text { formulae as these will be } \\ \text { discussed under a separate chapter }\end{array}\right\}\)

Here, \(\mathrm{G}=\) universal gravitation constant

$=6.67\times {{10}^{-11}}\frac{\text{N}-{{\text{m}}^{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ k}{{\text{g}}^{2}}}$

If a body of mass $m$ is raised to a height ' $h$ ' from the surface of earth, the change in potential energy of the system (earth + body) comes out to be :

\(\Delta \mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{mgh}}{\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)}(\mathrm{R}=\) radius of earth)

Or \(\Delta \mathrm{U} \approx mgh\) if \(h<<\mathrm{R}\)

Thus, the potential energy of a body at height h, i.e., mgh is really the change in potential energy of the system for $\text{h}<<\text{R}$. So be careful while using $\text{U}=\text{mgh}$, that $\text{h}$ should not be too large. This we will discuss in detail in the chapter of Gravitation.

concepts.title: Electric Potential Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential Energy

The electric potential energy of two point charges ${{q}_{1}}$ and ${{q}_{2}}$ separated by a distance $r$ in vacuum is given by

\(\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_1 \mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{r}}\left\{\begin{array}{l}\text { This part will be done in detail later } \\ \text { on in other chapter }\end{array}\right\}\)

Here, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}=9.0\times {{10}^{9}}\frac{\text{N}-{{\text{m}}^{2}}}{{{\text{C}}^{2}}}=$ constant

concepts.title: Important points about Potential Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Important points about Potential Energy

1.Change in potential energy is equal to the negative of work done by the conservative force $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ W} \right)$. If work done by the conservative force is negative change in potential energy will be positive or potential energy of the system will increase and vice-versa.

This can be understood by a simple example. Suppose a ball is taken from the ground to some height, work done by gravity is negative, i.e., change in potential energy should increase or potential energy of the ball will increase. Which happens so.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{W}}_{\text{gravity }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=-\text{ve} \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}=+\text{ve} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{\text{U}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{U}}_{\text{i}}}=+\text{ve} \\ \end{array}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}=-\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ W} \right)$

$\text{ F}=-\frac{\text{dU}}{\text{dr}}$, i.e., conservative forces always act in a direction where potential energy of the system is decreased. This can also be shown in Fig.

If a ball is dropped from a certain height. The force on it (its weight) acts in a direction in which its potential energy decreases.

2.Potential energy $U$ of a particle basically depends on its position, rather we can say its co-ordinates $\text{x},\text{y}$ and $\text{z}$. thus, potential energy of a particle is a function of its co-ordinates $\text{x},\text{y}$ and $\text{z}$ or

$\text{U}=\text{U}\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{z} \right)$

Now suppose potential energy function is known to us, then the force (of course conservative) acting on it is given by:

$\vec{F}=\left( \frac{\partial \text{U}}{\partial \text{x}}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+\frac{\partial \text{U}}{\partial \text{y}}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,+\frac{\partial \text{U}}{\partial \text{z}}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)$

Here $\frac{\partial U}{\partial \text{x}}=$ partial derivative of $\text{U}$ with respect to $\text{x}$. On the other hand, if the force acting on the particle is given to us and we want to find potential energy function, then we will use,

$dU=-\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}\text{ or }\mathop{\int }_{i}^{f}dU=-\mathop{\int }_{i}^{f}\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}$

But while using this equation, at least the potential energy of the particle at some reference point should be known to us. For example, if we are given that $U=0$ at $r=\propto$. Then

$\mathop{\int }_{\infty }^{\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{z} \right)}\text{dU}=-\mathop{\int }_{\infty }^{\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{z} \right)}\vec{F}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}$

or \(U(x, y, z)-U(\propto)=\int_{\infty}^{(x, y, z)} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}\) or \(U(x, y, z)=\int_{\propto}^{(x, y, z)} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}\)

as $\text{U}\left( \propto \right)=0$

Similarly, if we are given that $\text{U}={{\text{U}}_{0}}$ at origin. Then

\(\int_{(0,0,0)}^{(x, y, z)} d U=-\int_{(0,0,0)}^{(x, y, z)} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}\) or \(U(x, y, z)-U(0,0,0)=-\int_{(0,0,0)}^{(x, y, z)} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}\)

or $U\left( x,y,z \right)-{{U}_{0}}=-\int _{\left( 0,0,0 \right)}^{\left( x,y,z \right)}\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}$ or $U\left( x,y,z \right)={{U}_{0}}-\int _{\left( 0,0,0 \right)}^{\left( x,y,z \right)}\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}$

3.Suppose a particle is released from point $A$ with $u=0$

Friction is absent everywhere. Then velocity at $\text{B}$ will be

$\text{v}=\sqrt{2\text{gh}}$

(irrespective of the track it follows from A to B)

Here, $\text{h}={{\text{h}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{h}}_{\text{B}}}$

4. Suppose a car is moving with constant speed in the track as shown in figure. Then equations of motion are as under.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} mg-{{N}_{A}}=\frac{m{{v}^{2}}}{{{R}_{A}}} & \text{ or } & {{N}_{A}}=mg-\frac{m{{v}^{2}}}{{{R}_{A}}} \\ {{N}_{B}}-mg=\frac{m{{v}^{2}}}{{{R}_{B}}} & \text{ or } & {{N}_{B}}=mg+\frac{m{{v}^{2}}}{{{R}_{B}}} \\ \end{array}$

And ${{\text{N}}_{\text{C}}}=\text{mg}$

Thus, we can say ${{\text{N}}_{\text{B}}}>{{\text{N}}_{\text{C}}}>{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}$

concepts.title: Work energy Theorem and its equations
concepts.conceptBody:
Work energy Theorem and its equations

This theorem is a very important tool that relates the works to kinetic energy. According to this theorem

Work done by all the forces (conservative or non conservative, external or internal) acting on a particle or an object is equal to the change in kinetic energy of it.

${{\text{W}}_{\text{net }}}=\Delta\text{ K}\cdot \text{E}.={{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{K}}_{\text{i}}}$

Let ${{\vec{F}}_{1}},{{\vec{F}}_{2}}\ldots$ be the individual forces acting on a particle. The resultant force is $\vec{F}={{\vec{F}}_{1}}+{{\vec{F}}_{2}}+\ldots$ and the work done by the resultant force is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{W} & =\int \vec{F}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}=\int \left( {{{\vec{F}}}_{1}}+{{{\vec{F}}}_{2}}+\ldots \right)\cdot \text{d}\vec{r} \\ {} & =\int {{{\vec{F}}}_{1}}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}+\int {{{\vec{F}}}_{2}}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}+\ldots \\ \end{array}$

where $\int {{\vec{F}}_{1}}\cdot d\vec{r}$ is the work done on the particle by ${{\vec{F}}_{1}}$ and so on. Thus, work energy theorem can also be written as: work done by the resultant force is equal to the sum of the work done by the individual forces. 

Work-Energy Equations 

Let ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{c}}}$ be the conservative force, ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{nc}}}$ be non-conservative and ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{ex}}}$ be the external forces acting on a body, Then

$\frac{\text{md}\vec{v}}{\text{dt}}={{\vec{F}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\vec{F}}_{\text{nc}}}+{{\vec{F}}_{\text{ex}}}$ 

$m\left(\frac{d \vec{v}}{d t}\right) \cdot d \vec{r}=\vec{F}_c \cdot d \vec{r}+\vec{F}_{n c} \cdot d r+\vec{F}_{e x} \cdot d r$

$m(\vec{v} \cdot d \vec{v})=\vec{F}_c \cdot d \vec{r}+\vec{F}_{n c} \cdot d \vec{r}+\vec{F}_{e x} \cdot d r$

$\vec{v}\cdot d\vec{v}=\frac{d\left( \vec{v}\cdot \vec{v} \right)}{2}=\frac{d{{v}^{2}}}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \left( \frac{md{{v}^{2}}}{2} \right)={{\vec{F}}_{c}}\cdot d\vec{r}+{{\vec{F}}_{nc}}\cdot d\vec{r}+{{\vec{F}}_{\text{ex }}}\cdot dx$

Now, ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{c}}}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}=\text{d}{{\text{W}}_{\text{c}}}\{$ Work done by conservative forces $\}$

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{nc}}}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}=\text{d}{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}\left\{ \text{ Work }\text{ done }\text{ by }\text{ non-conservative }\text{ forces } \right\}$

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{ex}}}\cdot d \vec{r}=\text{d}{{\text{W}}_{\text{ex}}}\left\{ \text{ work }\text{ done }\text{ by }\text{ external } \right\}$

$\text{d}\left( \frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}} \right)=\text{d}{{\text{W}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\text{d}}_{\text{nc}}}+\text{d}{{\text{W}}_{\text{ex}}}$

$\therefore$ Now,

$\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}=\text{ Kinetic }\text{ Energy}$

$\therefore \int \text{dK}=\int \text{dW }\therefore \Delta\text{ K}=\Delta\text{ W (1)}$

$\Rightarrow $ Change in K.E. is equal to total work done in same frame of reference.

Now,

$\Delta\text{ K}=\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{c}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ex}}}$

$\Delta\text{ K}+\Delta\text{ u}=\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ex}}}\left\{ \because \Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{c}}}=-\Delta\text{ u} \right\}$

$\Delta\text{ u}$ is the change in internal energy of the block

$\therefore \Delta\text{ K}+\Delta\text{ u}=\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ex}}}$ (2)

If $\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ex}}}=0$

Then $\Delta\text{ K}+\Delta\text{ u}=0$

$\Rightarrow \left( {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{K}}_{\text{i}}} \right)+{{\text{u}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{u}}_{\text{i}}}=0$

$\Rightarrow \left( {{\text{K}}_{\text{i}}}+{{\text{u}}_{\text{i}}}={{\text{u}}_{\text{f}}}+{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \right)$ (3)

Three important work-energy equations [under different case] 
(1) $\Delta\text{ K}=\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{c}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ext}}}$ 
(2) $\Delta\text{ K}+\Delta\text{ u}=\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ext }}}\left[ \therefore \Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{c}}}=-\Delta\text{ u} \right]$ 
(3) $\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \Delta\text{ K}+\Delta\text{ u} & =0\left[ \text{ when    }\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{nc}}}+\Delta{{\text{W}}_{\text{ext }}}=0 \right] \\\end{array}$

concepts.title: Introduction and points to remember
concepts.conceptBody:
Conservation of Energy

The principle of conservation of energy state that the total energy of a system remains constant. Energy cannot be created or destroyed but may be converted from one form to another.

Take the case of a crate on a slope. Initially it is at rest, all its energy is potential energy. As it accelerates, some of it potential energy is converted into kinetic energy and some used to overcome friction. This energy used to overcome friction is not lost but converted into heat. At the bottom of the slope the energy will be purely kinetic (assuming the datum for potential energy is the bottom of the slope.) If we consider a body falling freely in air, neglecting air resistance, then mechanical energy is conserved , as potential energy is lost and equal amount of kinetic energy is gained as speed increases.

If the motion involves friction or collisions then the principle of conservation of energy is true, but conservation of mechanical energy is not applicable as some energy is converted to heat and perhaps sound.

concepts.title: Stable, Unstable and Neutral Equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Three Types of Equilibrium 

As we have studied in the chapter of 'Laws of motion' a body is said to be in translatory equilibrium if net force acting on the body is zero, i.e.,

${{\vec{F}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0$

if the forces are conservative $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }F=-\frac{dU}{dr}$

and for equilibrium $\text{F}=0$

So, $-\frac{\text{d}U}{\text{dr}}=0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{dU}}{\text{dr}}=0$

i.e., at equilibrium position slope of U-r graph is zero or the potential energy is optimum (maximum or minimum or constant). Equilibrium are of three types, i.e., the situation where $F=0$ and $\frac{dU}{dr}=0$ can be obtained under three conditions. These are stable equilibrium, unstable equilibrium and neutral equilibrium. These three types of equilibrium can be better understood from the following three figures:

Three identical balls are placed in equilibrium in positions as shown in figures (a), (b) and (c) respectively.

In fig. (a) ball is placed inside a smooth spherical shell. This ball is stable equilibrium position. In Fig. (b) the ball is placed over a smooth sphere. This is unstable equilibrium position. In Fig. (c) the ball is placed on a smooth horizontal ground. This ball is in neutral equilibrium position.

The table given below explains what is the difference and what are the similarities between these three equilibrium positions in the language of physics.

Note: 

1.If we plot graphs between $F$ and $r$ or $U$ and $r,F$ will be zero at equilibrium while $U$ will be maximum, minimum or constant depending on the type of equilibrium. This all is shown in Fig. 
 

At point $A,F=0,\frac{dU}{dr}=0$, but $U$ is constant. Hence, A is neutral equilibrium position. At points $\text{B}$ and $\text{D},\text{F}=0,\frac{\text{dU}}{\text{dr}}=0$ but $\text{U}$ is maximum. Thus, these are the points of unstable equilibrium.

At point $\text{C},\text{F}=0,\frac{\text{dU}}{\text{dr}}=0$, but $\text{U}$ is minimum. Hence, point $\text{C}$ is in stable equilibrium position.

2.Oscillations of a body take place about stable equilibrium position. For example, bob of a pendulum oscillates about its lowest point which is also the stable equilibrium position of bob. Similarly, in Fig. (b), the ball will oscillate about its stable equilibrium position.

A spring with spring constant $k$ is extended from $x=0_{\mathrm{x}=0 \text { to } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{x}_1} x=x_1$. The work done will be
The potential energy of a body is given by, $\quad U=A-B x^2$ (Where $x$ is the displacement). The magnitude of force acting on the particle is

A body of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is thrown up vertically with K.E. of 490 joules. If the acceleration due to gravity is $9.8 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then the height at which the K.E. of the body becomes half its original value is given by
If the $K . E$. of a body is increased by $300 \%$, its momentum will increase by
If a body looses half of its velocity on penetrating $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ in a wooden block, then how much will it penetrate more before coming to rest
